t: Symptoms resolve within 6 months after the stress factor (or its consequences) has terminated (DSM-5-TR).
DSM-5-TR — onset at 3 months; subtype specifiers.
ICD-11 — onset 1 month; 'preoccupation with the stressor' main symptom; no subtypes, but additional anxiety qualifiers.
Clinical interview — stressor, symptom onset, prior psychiatric history.
MDD, GAD, PTSD, complex PTSD exclusion.
From a close loss ≥ 6 months (ICD-11) / 12 months (DSM-5-TR).
— supportive, problem-solving, CBT-based brief intervention (8–12 sessions).
Stress management, strengthening social support, development of coping strategies.
Pharmacotherapy — only in severe cases or comorbid; SSRIs short-term; benzodiazepines for acute anxiety for 1–2 weeks.
Removal or modification of the stress factor (if possible).
NICE — no separate guideline for adjustment disorder; general anxiety and depression principles.
WHO mhGAP — supportive approach in acute stress reactions, 'watchful waiting' in mild cases.
Evidence base is limited — diagnostic residual nature.
Short-term problem-solving therapy (Problem-Solving Therapy) – Nezu (Nezu A.M.)
4–8 sessions; concrete problem identification, evaluation of solution options, behavioral execution.
— Breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, mindfulness.
In most cases remission within 6 months.
Some persist or transition to MDD/PTSD — follow-up recommended.
Myth 1: "Adjustment disorder is not a real diagnosis, just 'life difficulty'"
Clinical diagnosis requires functional impairment or acute distress; suicidal risk must be assessed especially.
Myth 2: "When the stressor passes, the diagnosis automatically resolves, no treatment needed"
Some cases persist or transition to MDD/PTSD; short-term intervention prevents treatment transition.
Myth 3: "Antidepressant is first-line for adjustment disorder"
Psychotherapy is first-line; pharmacotherapy only in severe or comorbid cases.
Myth 4: "Suicide risk is low in adjustment disorder"
Acute stress reactions are accompanied by a high impulsivity suicide risk; active assessment.
Myth 5: "The patient is weak or has a 'weak character'"
Reaction result of interaction between stress intensity and coping resources; weakness of character explanation stigmatizing and incorrect.
Maercker A. et al. ICD-11 stress-related disorders. World Psychiatry 2013;12(3):198–206.
Casey P. Adjustment disorder: epidemiology, diagnosis and treatment. CNS Drugs 2009;23(11):927–938.
